answer:
**Manusmṛti** (Book 1) presents cosmogenesis as an emanation from a singular divine source—the Self-Existent One (Svayambhu), who manifests from primordial darkness. This entity creates waters via contemplation, deposits seed into them forming a golden egg, from which Brahma (the progenitor) emerges; Brahma then produces mind (described as "of the nature of both being and non-being"), ego, senses, elements, and all beings in sequence, culminating in social orders and cosmic cycles.[^1][^2]

**Vṛhaspatitattva** analyzes the cosmos via a dual ontology: all *tattvas* (principles of reality) arise when *cetana* (Conscious Principle: eternally aware, unobscured knowledge, subdivided into *Paramaśiva*, *Sadāśiva*, and *Śiva*) meets *acetana* (Unconscious Principle: inert, like stone). Their conjunction yields impure principles (*pradhāna*, *tri-guṇa*/*sattva-rajas-tamas*, *buddhi*, *ahaṅkāra*, senses, subtle/gross elements), binding souls (*ātmā*) into embodiment and rebirth until yogic realization restores pure *cetana*.[^3]

Manu's narrative emanation—from unmanifest unity through dual-natured mind to differentiated cosmos—maps more seamlessly onto Vṛhaspatitattva's *cetana*-*acetana* hierarchy and *tattva*-sequence (e.g., Manu's "being/non-being" mind ~ *cetana*-*acetana* interplay; elements/senses ~ lower *tattvas*; Brahma ~ impure *cetana*). Vṛhaspatitattva's stark primordial dualism, however, strains Manu's monistic Self-Existent origin (no explicit *cetana*-*acetana* bifurcation), risking dilution of its analytical purity into mythic singularity.[^1][^3]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 1, [¶6-9,11](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-93cef0).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 1, [¶14](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-f689c4).
[^3]: Vṛhaspatitattva, Ch. 1, [¶58-59](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-04d69c).
